title: 16-year-old girl with polyuria, polydipsia and weight loss
candidateInstructions: You are an FY1 doctor in the ED. Mia Chandler, a 16-year-old student, is brought by her parents with a 3-week history of excessive thirst, frequent urination, and weight loss. She is alert and oriented. Her blood glucose on point-of-care testing is 24.2 mmol/L. Please take a history and explain your initial management.
examinerNotes: New presentation of Type 1 diabetes mellitus with likely DKA (glucose 24.2, likely ketotic). Key history: classic triad polyuria/polydipsia/weight loss, new onset in teenager, NO history of diabetes. Candidate must recognise severity, request ketones/ABG, and start DKA protocol.

patient.name: Mia Chandler
patient.presentingComplaint: I've been drinking huge amounts, going to the toilet all the time, and losing weight for 3 weeks
patient.reasonForAttendance: My mum brought me because she's really worried — I lost 5kg and I've been so thirsty

hpi.character: Excessive thirst drinking 6+ litres/day, urinating every 1-2 hours day and night (nocturia), unintentional weight loss
hpi.timing: Progressive over 3 weeks, tummy pain new in last 2 days
hpi.severity: Tummy pain 5/10. Fatigue stopping normal activities.
hpi.previousEpisodes: Never had anything like this before

systemsReview.respiratory: Feeling short of breath in last 2 days
systemsReview.endocrine: Weight loss, extreme thirst — classic hyperglycaemia

examination.general: Thin teenager, mild fruity breath odour (ketones), slightly tachypnoeic
examination.cardiovascular: Tachycardia, dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor

investigations.name: Blood Ketones
investigations.interpretation: Severe ketonaemia (>3.0 mmol/L = severe DKA). Urgent DKA protocol required.

investigations.name: Arterial Blood Gas
investigations.interpretation: SEVERE METABOLIC ACIDOSIS with respiratory compensation (Kussmaul breathing). pH 7.18 = severe DKA. Anion gap markedly elevated.

investigations.name: Urea & Electrolytes
investigations.interpretation: Hyperkalemia (shifts from intracellular during acidosis — total body K is depleted). Mild hyponatraemia (dilutional). Pre-renal AKI. Potassium must be monitored closely during insulin therapy.

patient.emotionalState: Scared teenager. Trying to be brave. Clearly unwell. Parents are very anxious.
patient.communicationStyle: Quiet, answers briefly. Slightly confused about what has been happening. Parents speaking over her at times.
hiddenConcerns: Am I going to need injections forever?
My aunt has diabetes — is this what I have?
doNotVolunteer: Blurred vision — only if asked about eyes
Tummy pain — only if asked about abdominal symptoms
Family history of T1DM — only if asked about family history of diabetes or medical conditions
Shortness of breath — only if specifically asked

primaryDiagnosis: New-onset Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus presenting in Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA)
differentialDiagnoses: MODY (Maturity Onset Diabetes of the Young)

dataGathering.detailedExpectation: Polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss — all three

dataGathering.criterion: Identifies DKA features — nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain
dataGathering.detailedExpectation: Asks about nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain — DKA precipitants
Orders ketone measurement to diagnose DKA
Orders arterial blood gas to assess severity of acidosis

dataGathering.criterion: Asks about family history of diabetes
dataGathering.detailedExpectation: Family history of T1DM or autoimmune conditions

clinicalManagement.detailedExpectation: States DKA diagnosis and initiates insulin/fluids/electrolytes
Aware of potassium shifts during insulin therapy — hypokalaemia risk

interpersonalSkills.detailedExpectation: Speaks to teenager directly, not just through parents

references.source: JBDS-IP: Management of DKA in adults 2023
NICE NG18: Type 1 diabetes in adults

learningObjectives: Recognise the classic triad of T1DM presentation
Identify DKA based on clinical features
Order appropriate investigations for DKA diagnosis and monitoring
